site_tagline: All Calculators Online — Math, Finance, Health, Science & More
home_h1: All Calculators Online — Math, Finance, Health, Science & More
site_description: Over 460 free online calculators: math, finance, health, statistics, science, conversions, sports, construction and more. Instant results, no registration.

block_descriptions.estructuras: Free foundation and concrete calculators. Calculate cement, sand, gravel, rebar and formwork for slabs, footings and structural elements.
block_descriptions.mamposteria: Free masonry calculators for bricks, blocks and mortar. Get exact quantities for walls, partitions and enclosures including wastage.
block_descriptions.pavimentos: Free flooring and tiling calculators. Calculate tiles, parquet, adhesive, grout and wastage for any room size and tile format.
block_descriptions.fontaneria: Free plumbing and water calculators. Estimate pipe sizes, water pressure, flow rates and drainage for residential projects.
block_descriptions.electricidad: Free electrical and lighting calculators. Calculate lumens, BTU, circuit loads and wiring for homes and commercial spaces.
block_descriptions.climatizacion: Free HVAC and climate control calculators. Estimate AC capacity, radiator output, insulation R-value and heating loads.
block_descriptions.carpinteria: Free carpentry and metalwork calculators. Calculate doors, windows, stairs, metal sections and structural timber.
block_descriptions.pintura: Free paint and finish calculators. Calculate litres of paint, rolls of wallpaper, plaster and rendering for any surface.
block_descriptions.gestion: Free construction management calculators. Estimate project costs, labour hours, material budgets and renovation timelines.
block_descriptions.matematicas: Free math calculators: percentages, geometry, algebra, rule of three and more. Instant results, no sign-up required.
block_descriptions.finanzas: Free financial calculators: mortgage, loan, compound interest, VAT, net salary, discount and break-even point.
block_descriptions.salud: Free health calculators: BMI, daily calories, ideal weight and water intake. Results based on your weight, height and activity level.
block_descriptions.cotidiano: Everyday life calculators: tip, age, date difference and more. Handy tools for any daily situation.
block_descriptions.estadistica: Free statistics calculators: mean, median, standard deviation, variance, probability and more.
block_descriptions.ciencia: Science and physics calculators: speed, force, energy, pressure, electricity and more.
block_descriptions.conversion: Free unit converters: length, weight, temperature, volume, area, speed and more.
block_descriptions.deportes: Sports calculators: running pace, calories burned, heart rate, VO2 max and more.

error_invalid_math: Cannot calculate — check your inputs (division by zero or out-of-range value).
private_mode_warning: Private browsing — your inputs won't be saved between visits.
consent_required_lead: Accept cookies to receive updates by email.
sensitivity_no_var: Add a second input to see how the result changes.
email_consent: I agree to receive product updates by email.
footer_disclaimer: Results are approximate estimates. Always consult a professional.

100.name: Tool ROI Calculator
100.desc: Calculate return on investment for a tool purchase.
100.seo_title: Tool ROI Calculator – Return on Investment in Construction
100.seo_desc: Calculate if buying that tool pays off: monthly savings, months to recover investment (payback), and total ROI over useful life. Free.
100.seo_description: Calculate return on investment (ROI) for construction tools and equipment. Determine how many months to recover the investment and the total savings over the entire useful life of the equipment.
100.example_label: Example calculation for Tool ROI Calculator using the default values.
100.result_context: Resultados: ahorro mensual de {ahorro_mensual_eur} €/mes, payback de {payback_meses} meses, ahorro total de {ahorro_total_eur} €, ROI del {roi_pct} %.
100.formula_display: ROI = (ahorro_total − inversión) ÷ inversión × 100. Payback = inversión ÷ ahorro_mensual
steps: Identify what you want to calculate with the Tool ROI Calculator.
Enter the tool cost (â‚¬) in the input field.
Enter the hours saved per month in the input field.
Enter the labour cost per hour (â‚¬) in the input field.
Fill in the remaining fields with your measurements.
Click Calculate to see your result instantly.
mistakes: Entering values in the wrong units — check that all measurements use the same unit system.
Forgetting to include a waste or safety margin — add 5-10% extra for real-world projects.
Using incorrect default values — verify the suggested defaults match your specific situation.
Rounding intermediate values too early — keep full precision until the final result.

043.name: Cable Cross-Section Calculator
043.desc: Calculate copper cable cross-section in mm² based on current, length, and voltage drop limits per IEC 60364. Free, no sign-up, instant results.
043.seo_title: Cable Size Calculator – mm² Cross-Section – IEC 60364
043.seo_description: Calculate cable cross-section in mm² based on current, length, voltage and allowable voltage drop. Free, accurate, instant results with formula guide.
043.example_label: Size cable cross-section for a single-phase 16 A circuit with 20 m length at 230 V and 3 % maximum voltage drop.

range_hints.corriente_a: Circuit rated current; typically 10-32 A for residential use, up to 200 A for industrial service entrances
range_hints.longitud_m: Actual cable length from distribution board to farthest load, not straight-line distance
range_hints.caida_max_pct: Maximum 3 % for lighting circuits per IEC; 5 % for power circuits. In rural areas with weak grid, reduce to 2 %

043.result_context: A {seccion_normalizada_mm2} mm² copper cable is required. The exact calculated section is {seccion_calc_mm2} mm², producing an actual voltage drop of {caida_real_pct} %. If above 5 %, increase to the next standard size.
043.formula_display: S = (2 × L × I) ÷ (σ × ΔV) with σ = 56 for copper. ΔV = V × drop% ÷ 100. Round up to next standard gauge: 1.5, 2.5, 4, 6, 10, 16, 25, or 35 mm².
steps: Determine design current (Ib) from the connected load: Ib = Power (W) ÷ Voltage (V)
Calculate allowable voltage drop: ΔV = Voltage × max drop % ÷ 100. For 230 V at 3 %: 230 × 0.03 = 6.9 V
Apply the section formula for single-phase copper: S = (2 × L × I) ÷ (56 × ΔV). Example with 20 m and 16 A: S = (2 × 20 × 16) ÷ (56 × 6.9) = 1.66 mm²
Round up to the next standard gauge. For 1.66 mm², the commercial size is 2.5 mm²
Verify actual voltage drop with the standard section: actual drop % = (2 × L × I) ÷ (56 × S_std × V) × 100. If it exceeds the limit, go up one size
mistakes: Ignoring voltage drop in long circuits: a 4 mm² cable carrying 25 A over 80 m loses 24.5 V (10.6 %), damaging connected equipment and wasting energy
Using aluminum without increasing section: aluminum has only 61 % of copper's conductivity; replacing 6 mm² copper requires 16 mm² aluminum
Bundling too many cables in one conduit: 6 × 2.5 mm² cables in a 25 mm conduit reduce each cable's ampacity to 64 % of its nominal rating
Not applying ambient temperature correction factors: at 50 °C a 2.5 mm² cable drops from 21 A to 14.9 A (factor 0.71)
Connecting aluminum to copper without bimetallic terminals: galvanic corrosion increases joint resistance from 0.001 Ω to 0.5-1.0 Ω within 12-18 months
043.trust_note: Copper conductivity at 20 °C is taken as σ = 56 m/(Ω·mm²). For aluminum reduce to 61 %. Standard cross-sections follow the series 1.5, 2.5, 4, 6, 10, 16, 25, 35, 50, 70, 95, 120 mm² per IEC 60228. For ambient temperatures above 40 °C apply derating factors per IEC 60364-5-52.
043.standard: IEC 60364-5-52 for low-voltage electrical installations. Maximum voltage drop is 3 % for lighting and 5 % for power circuits relative to nominal voltage. For North America, refer to NEC Article 210.19(A) with a combined 5 % limit for feeders and branch circuits.

input_type_review.reason: Correct: continuous numeric value
Correct: continuous numeric value
Correct: discrete option
Correct: continuous numeric value

044.name: Voltage Drop Calculator
044.desc: Check if your cable's voltage drop meets IEC 60364 limits. Enter section, length, current and voltage. Free, no sign-up, instant results.
044.seo_title: Voltage Drop Calculator – % and Volts – IEC 60364
044.seo_description: Check if your cable's voltage drop meets IEC 60364 limits. Enter section, length, current and voltage. Free, no sign-up, instant results.
044.example_label: Check voltage drop in a 2.5 mm² copper cable, 30 m long, carrying 16 A at 230 V single-phase.

range_hints.seccion_mm2: Installed conductor section: 1.5, 2.5, 4, 6, 10, 16, 25, 35, 50, 70, 95 or 120 mm² per IEC 60228
range_hints.longitud_m: One-way cable length from supply to load. For single-phase circuits consider both outbound and return path
range_hints.corriente_a: Steady-state load current. Do not confuse with short-circuit current

044.result_context: With a {seccion_mm2} mm² cable, {longitud_m} m long, carrying {corriente_a} A at {tension_v} V, the drop is {caida_v} V ({caida_pct} %). Compliance: {cumple_rebt}. Limit is 3 % for lighting, 5 % for power circuits.
044.formula_display: ΔU = (2 × L × I) ÷ (σ × S) for single-phase; ΔU% = (ΔU ÷ V) × 100. With σ = 56 m/(Ω·mm²) for copper at 20 °C. If the result exceeds the limit, increase conductor section.
steps: Identify installed cable section in mm² (marking on cable jacket or rating plate)
Measure actual cable length from panel to farthest load; do not use straight-line distance
Calculate percentage: ΔU% = (6.86 ÷ 230) × 100 = 2.98 %. Compare: 3 % for lighting, 5 % for power
mistakes: Using straight-line distance instead of actual cable length: a routed cable with bends adds 15-30 % extra length, invalidating the calculation
Applying single-phase formula to three-phase: for three-phase ΔU = (√3 × L × I) ÷ (σ × S), which reduces drop by 13 %
Forgetting that copper conductivity decreases with temperature: at 70 °C σ drops to 47 m/(Ω·mm²), 16 % less than at 20 °C
Not checking cumulative voltage drop across the entire installation: IEC limits apply from the supply origin to any utilization point
044.trust_note: Calculation based on annealed electrolytic copper conductivity at 20 °C (σ = 56 m/(Ω·mm²), 100 % IACS). For aluminum use σ = 35 m/(Ω·mm²). At elevated operating temperatures the actual σ value decreases, increasing voltage drop.
044.standard: IEC 60364-5-52: 3 % for lighting circuits, 5 % for power circuits. NEC Article 210.19(A) limits combined feeder and branch circuit drop to 5 % in North America. Measurement from installation origin to the farthest utilization point.

range_hints.area_m2: Conditioned floor area. Home: 80-200 m², Passive house: same area, much lower demand

steps: Calculate heating demand: 120 m² × 50 W/m² = 6000 W = 6.0 kW
Calculate DHW demand: 4 people × 0.2 kW = 0.8 kW

Select a 7-8 kW commercial heat pump unit
mistakes: Not considering outdoor design temperature — COP drops significantly below freezing (< -5°C)

Overlooking backup electric heater — many heat pumps include resistance backup for extreme days

078.formula_display: Presupuesto = Σ(partidas × precio_unitario) + IVA + Gastos Generales
steps: Enumera todas las partidas de obra (demolición, albañilería, fontanería, electricidad, acabados...)
Mide las cantidades de cada partida (m², m, ud) según planos del proyecto
Asigna el precio unitario de cada partida según la base de precios de construcción vigente
Multiplica cada partida por su precio unitario y suma todos los subtotales
Añade el porcentaje de gastos generales (13-19%) y beneficio industrial (6%)
Aplica el IVA correspondiente (21% para obras, 10% para rehabilitación en vivienda habitual)
Suma honorarios de proyecto (arquitecto, aparejador) si procede

steps: Cuenta el número total de trabajadores en obra que necesitan EPIs
Determina la duración de la obra en meses para calcular los recambios necesarios
Selecciona el tipo de obra: reforma (riesgo estándar), estructura (riesgo medio-alto), tejado (riesgo alto de caída), demolición (riesgo máximo)
Calcula el coste inicial: nº trabajadores × 45 € base × coeficiente de riesgo de la actividad
Estima los recambios: 10% del coste inicial por cada mes de obra (cascos, guantes, mascarillas se desgastan)
Revisa el listado de EPIs obligatorios: casco, guantes, calzado de seguridad, chaleco reflectante, protección auditiva y respiratoria según actividad
mistakes: No proporcionar EPIs a todos los trabajadores: es obligación del empresario según Ley de Prevención de Riesgos Laborales
Usar EPIs caducados: los cascos y arneses tienen fecha de caducidad (5 años desde fabricación)
No reemplazar EPIs dañados: un casco que ha recibido un impacto debe sustituirse inmediatamente
Olvidar EPIs específicos para tareas especiales: soldadura, corte, manejo de productos químicos
Comprar EPIs sin marcado CE: deben cumplir el Reglamento (UE) 2016/425

input_type_review.reason: Tipo correcto: numérico entero para el conteo de trabajadores.

steps: Enumera el número de tareas del proyecto (según el desglose EDT o plan de obra)
Asigna la duración media estimada por tarea considerando la complejidad y recursos disponibles

mistakes: Planificar todas las tareas en serie: la construcción permite muchos solapes (instalaciones mientras secan soleras, etc.)

Olvidar los tiempos de curado del hormigón (7-28 días según elemento estructural) en la secuencia de tareas
No considerar los periodos de vacaciones, festivos locales y días de climatología adversa según la zona
Confundir días laborables con días naturales: el plazo de ejecución suele referirse a días laborables

094.name: Equipment Loan Calculator

steps: Define el importe a financiar: coste de adquisición del equipo o maquinaria (hormigonera, miniexcavadora, furgoneta...)
Introduce la TAE (Tasa Anual Equivalente) de la financiera: entre 5-10% TAE para préstamos de equipos de construcción
Establece el plazo de financiación en meses: 24-60 meses para herramientas, 48-120 meses para maquinaria pesada
Aplica la fórmula de amortización francés (cuotas constantes): r = TAE/12, n = plazo en meses
Calcula el total pagado = cuota × plazo y los intereses totales = total pagado − importe solicitado
Compara la opción de préstamo con el renting o leasing (cuota mensual sin propiedad final del bien)
mistakes: Fijarse solo en la cuota mensual y no en el coste total del préstamo (intereses + comisiones)
No comparar TAE entre entidades: la TAE incluye comisiones y gastos, es la referencia real del coste
Elegir plazos demasiado largos: pagas menos al mes pero muchos más intereses totales
Olvidar que los intereses del préstamo son gasto deducible en la actividad profesional o empresarial
No considerar que la maquinaria financiada puede amortizarse fiscalmente de forma acelerada (libertad de amortización para pymes)
